quiz_questions.question_text: Nonparametric tests are used when:

options.text: Data are always normal
Normality assumptions are violated, data are ordinal, or samples are small
Parametric tests are unavailable in software
The sample size is very large

quiz_questions.explanation: Nonparametric tests are chosen when parametric assumptions (like normality) do not hold, when data are measured on an ordinal scale, or when sample sizes are too small for the Central Limit Theorem to help.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test is a nonparametric alternative to:

options.text: ANOVA
Chi-square test
The paired t-test
The z-test

quiz_questions.explanation: The Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test tests paired differences without assuming the differences are normally distributed.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Mann-Whitney U Test compares:

options.text: Means of three or more groups
Paired observations
Two independent groups using ranks
Variances of two groups

quiz_questions.explanation: The Mann-Whitney U Test compares the rank distributions between two independent samples to determine if they come from the same population.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Kruskal-Wallis test is the nonparametric alternative to:

options.text: Paired t-test
Chi-square test
One-way ANOVA
Two-sample t-test

quiz_questions.explanation: The Kruskal-Wallis test compares distributions across three or more independent groups without requiring normality, serving as the nonparametric version of one-way ANOVA.

quiz_questions.question_text: Most nonparametric tests use ___ instead of raw data values:

options.text: Means
Variances
Ranks
Medians only

quiz_questions.explanation: Most nonparametric tests convert raw data to ranks, which reduces the influence of outliers and removes the need for distributional assumptions.

quiz_questions.question_text: The main trade-off with nonparametric tests is:

options.text: They are always better than parametric tests
Less statistical power than parametric tests when parametric assumptions are met
They require more assumptions than parametric tests
They cannot handle ordinal data

quiz_questions.explanation: When parametric assumptions hold (especially normality), parametric tests are more powerful. Nonparametric tests sacrifice some power for greater flexibility.

quiz_questions.question_text: Spearman's rank correlation (r_s) measures:

options.text: Only linear association
Monotonic association (consistently increasing or decreasing)
Causation between variables
Only positive relationships

quiz_questions.explanation: Spearman's r_s captures any monotonic relationship (consistently increasing or decreasing), not just linear ones. It is Pearson's r applied to the ranked data.

quiz_questions.question_text: If data are normally distributed with a large sample, the preferred approach is:

options.text: Use nonparametric tests for safety
Parametric tests, because they have more power when assumptions are met
Both give identical results, so it does not matter
Neither test works for normal data

quiz_questions.explanation: When parametric assumptions are satisfied, parametric tests have greater statistical power to detect true effects.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Friedman test is the nonparametric alternative to:

options.text: One-way ANOVA
The z-test
Repeated-measures ANOVA (within-subjects)
Simple linear regression

quiz_questions.explanation: The Friedman test handles ranked data from repeated measures (the same subjects measured under multiple conditions), serving as the nonparametric version of repeated-measures ANOVA.

quiz_questions.question_text: If a distribution is heavily right-skewed with a small sample, the best approach is:

options.text: Use a t-test anyway
Consider a nonparametric test
Assume normality regardless
Increase the mean to correct the skew

quiz_questions.explanation: With small n and clear non-normality, nonparametric methods are preferred because the t-test's normality assumption is not met, and the CLT cannot compensate with such a small sample.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'distribution-free test'?

options.text: A test that only works with the normal distribution
Another name for nonparametric tests, because they do not require specific distributional assumptions
A test that has no power
A test that ignores all data structure

quiz_questions.explanation: Nonparametric tests are called 'distribution-free' because they do not assume the data come from any particular probability distribution family.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'sign test'?

options.text: A test that uses all data values and their magnitudes
A simple nonparametric test that considers only the signs (+ or -) of differences, ignoring magnitudes
A test for correlation between variables
A parametric test requiring normally distributed data

quiz_questions.explanation: The sign test uses only the direction of differences (positive or negative), not their magnitude. It is one of the simplest nonparametric tests and is very robust but less powerful than the Wilcoxon signed-rank test.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'statistical power' mean?

options.text: The probability of a Type I error
The probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis
The sample size needed for a study
The strength of the effect being studied

quiz_questions.explanation: Statistical power = 1 - P(Type II error). It is the probability of detecting a true effect when one exists. Higher power means a better chance of finding real differences.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'bootstrapping' mean in statistics?

options.text: Using theoretical distributions to compute p-values
A resampling method that draws repeated samples with replacement from observed data to estimate the sampling distribution
A method that requires normality assumptions
The same procedure as the t-test

quiz_questions.explanation: Bootstrapping is a modern nonparametric approach that builds an empirical sampling distribution by repeatedly resampling from the observed data with replacement, requiring no distributional assumptions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'asymptotic relative efficiency' of a nonparametric test?

options.text: It is always 100%
A ratio comparing the sample size needed by a nonparametric test to achieve the same power as its parametric counterpart
The speed at which the test runs on a computer
A measure of how fast the p-value approaches zero

quiz_questions.explanation: Asymptotic relative efficiency compares nonparametric to parametric tests. For example, the Mann-Whitney test has about 95.5% efficiency relative to the t-test under normality, meaning it needs only slightly more data to achieve the same power.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'ordinal data' mean?

options.text: Data with exact numerical values and equal intervals
Data that can be ranked in order but the differences between ranks are not necessarily equal
Data that is always normally distributed
Data collected in chronological order

quiz_questions.explanation: Ordinal data has a natural order (e.g., pain ratings: mild, moderate, severe) but the intervals between categories are not necessarily equal. Nonparametric tests are ideal for ordinal data.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'Wilcoxon Rank-Sum Test'?

options.text: Another name for the Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test
Equivalent to the Mann-Whitney U Test; it compares two independent groups using ranks
A test for comparing three or more groups
A parametric test for paired data

quiz_questions.explanation: The Wilcoxon Rank-Sum Test and the Mann-Whitney U Test are mathematically equivalent tests for comparing two independent groups using ranked data.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'robust to outliers' mean?

options.text: The test removes all outliers before analysis
The test's results are not heavily influenced by extreme values
The test requires outliers to function correctly
The test is only valid when there are no outliers

quiz_questions.explanation: Nonparametric tests are robust to outliers because they use ranks rather than raw values. An extreme outlier gets the highest or lowest rank, but its extreme magnitude does not disproportionately affect the test statistic.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'permutation test' refer to?

options.text: A parametric test based on the normal distribution
A nonparametric test that calculates the test statistic for all possible rearrangements of the data to build a reference distribution
A test that only works with categorical data
A method for computing Pearson's correlation

quiz_questions.explanation: Permutation tests shuffle group labels among all observations to generate the null distribution, then compare the observed statistic to this distribution. No distributional assumptions are needed.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the key advantage of Spearman's r_s over Pearson's r?

options.text: Spearman's r_s is always larger than Pearson's r
Spearman's r_s detects any monotonic relationship and does not require linearity or normality
Spearman's r_s only works with large samples
Spearman's r_s measures causation while Pearson's r does not

quiz_questions.explanation: Spearman's r_s ranks both variables and computes the Pearson correlation on the ranks. This captures monotonic (not just linear) relationships and is resistant to outliers and non-normality.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physical therapist rates patient pain on a scale of 1-10 before and after treatment for 15 patients. The differences are not normally distributed. Which test should she use to determine if treatment reduces pain?

options.text: Independent-samples t-test
Paired t-test
Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test
Chi-square test

quiz_questions.explanation: The data are paired (before/after for the same patients), the pain scale is ordinal, and the differences are not normal. The Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test is the appropriate nonparametric alternative to the paired t-test.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marketing team wants to compare customer satisfaction ratings (on a 5-point Likert scale) between online and in-store shoppers. The data are ordinal and the distributions are highly skewed. With 25 online and 30 in-store shoppers, which test is most appropriate?

options.text: Two-sample t-test
Mann-Whitney U Test
Paired t-test
One-way ANOVA

quiz_questions.explanation: Two independent groups, ordinal data, and non-normal distributions all point to the Mann-Whitney U Test as the appropriate choice.

quiz_questions.question_text: A food scientist ranks the taste quality of bread made with four different flour types. Each of 10 panelists rates all four types. The data are ordinal and the panelists rate all conditions. Which test should be used?

options.text: Kruskal-Wallis test
Mann-Whitney U Test
Friedman test
Chi-square test of independence

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a repeated-measures design (each panelist rates all four flour types) with ordinal data. The Friedman test is the nonparametric alternative to repeated-measures ANOVA.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher measures income (in dollars) for three education groups (high school, bachelor's, graduate). Income distributions are heavily right-skewed with outliers. Each group has about 20 participants. Which test should the researcher use?

options.text: One-way ANOVA
Kruskal-Wallis test
Mann-Whitney U Test
Friedman test

quiz_questions.explanation: Three independent groups with heavily skewed data and outliers call for the Kruskal-Wallis test, which is the nonparametric alternative to one-way ANOVA and is robust to non-normality.

quiz_questions.question_text: A biologist studies the relationship between tree age (years) and trunk diameter (cm) for 30 trees. Both variables are measured but the relationship appears curved (monotonic but not linear). Which correlation measure is better?

options.text: Pearson's r, because it handles any relationship
Spearman's r_s, because it measures monotonic relationships without requiring linearity
Chi-square, because the data are continuous
Neither correlation applies to biological data

quiz_questions.explanation: Spearman's r_s captures monotonic relationships regardless of whether the relationship is linear. Since tree age and diameter increase together but not at a constant rate, Spearman's r_s is more appropriate than Pearson's r.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher has 8 patients' blood pressure readings before and after a new medication. The sample is too small to verify normality. She uses the sign test and finds 7 out of 8 patients had lower blood pressure after treatment. Under H-null (no effect), what is the probability of 7 or more successes out of 8?
quiz_questions.explanation: Under H-null, each difference is equally likely to be + or - (p = 0.5). P(X >= 7) = P(X=7) + P(X=8) = C(8,7)(0.5)^8 + C(8,8)(0.5)^8 = (8 + 1)/256 = 9/256 = 0.035.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sports analyst uses bootstrapping to estimate the 95% confidence interval for the median marathon finish time from a sample of 50 runners. He resamples 10,000 times. What does the bootstrap confidence interval represent?

options.text: The exact range of all marathon times
A range of plausible values for the population median, constructed from the empirical sampling distribution
The probability that any single runner finishes in that range
A range that only applies to these 50 specific runners

quiz_questions.explanation: The bootstrap confidence interval uses the distribution of medians from 10,000 resamples to create a range of plausible values for the true population median, without requiring normality assumptions.

quiz_questions.question_text: A quality inspector compares defect severity ratings (none, minor, major, critical) across products from three factories. Each factory contributes independent samples. The data are ordinal. Which test is appropriate?

options.text: One-way ANOVA
Paired t-test
Kruskal-Wallis test
Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test

quiz_questions.explanation: Three independent groups with ordinal data (severity ratings) require a nonparametric test for comparing multiple groups. The Kruskal-Wallis test is the correct choice.

quiz_questions.question_text: A dataset has 12 paired observations. The Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test yields a test statistic of W = 10 with a critical value of 14 for alpha = 0.05 (two-tailed, where W must be <= critical value to reject). What is the conclusion?

options.text: Fail to reject H-null because W = 10 is too small
Reject H-null because W = 10 is less than or equal to the critical value of 14
The test is invalid because the sample size is too small
More data are needed to draw a conclusion

quiz_questions.explanation: For the Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test, smaller values of W provide evidence against H-null. Since W = 10 <= 14 (the critical value), we reject H-null at alpha = 0.05.

quiz_questions.question_text: A psychologist wants to test whether anxiety scores differ between a control group and a treatment group. The control group (n = 12) has scores with a severe outlier of 98 (all others range 15-45). The treatment group (n = 14) has normally distributed scores ranging 10-35. Which test should she use and why?

options.text: Independent t-test because both groups have more than 10 observations
Mann-Whitney U Test because the outlier in the control group would distort the t-test but has minimal impact on a rank-based test
Paired t-test because the groups are being compared
ANOVA because there are two groups

quiz_questions.explanation: The extreme outlier (98) would inflate the control group mean and standard deviation, distorting a t-test. The Mann-Whitney U Test uses ranks, so the outlier simply receives the highest rank without disproportionately affecting the result.
